primary_diagnosis.name: Orthostatic (postural) hypotension, likely iatrogenic from recently increased alpha-blocker (tamsulosin)

pathophysiology_summary: Orthostatic (postural) hypotension is a sustained fall in blood pressure on standing (conventionally >=20 mmHg systolic or >=10 mmHg diastolic within 3 minutes), causing transient cerebral hypoperfusion experienced as lightheadedness, visual darkening/greying, weakness and presyncope, relieved promptly by sitting or lying down as perfusion is restored. A 'coat-hanger' ache across the neck and shoulders (hypoperfusion of the postural neck muscles) is a fairly specific accompanying symptom. Normally, standing shifts blood into the lower body and the baroreflex triggers sympathetic vasoconstriction and a rise in heart rate to maintain cerebral perfusion; alpha-1 adrenergic blockers such as tamsulosin (used for benign prostatic hyperplasia) blunt this vasoconstriction, and a recent dose increase is a classic reversible precipitant - the modest compensatory heart-rate rise here (72 to 88 bpm) is consistent with impaired vasoconstriction rather than an autonomic failure or a bleed. The reproducible, strictly postural pattern with a measurable BP drop distinguishes it from vasovagal syncope (situational, with a prodrome and often paradoxical bradycardia), cardiac arrhythmia (symptoms not purely postural, with an irregular pulse), and BPPV/vertigo (true rotational spinning, no BP drop). The dominant risk is a traumatic fall, especially in an older person living alone with home hazards. Management is to withhold/adjust the offending drug, expand intravascular volume (hydration, temporary salt liberalisation), teach counter-manoeuvres (rising slowly, calf pumping) and reduce fall hazards, with prompt review. Iatrogenic orthostatic hypotension typically resolves within 24-48 hours of stopping the drug. Confirmatory orthostatic vitals and injury assessment require in-person review, but the offending drug can be safely withheld and fall-prevention advised over telehealth.

differential.evidence_basis: Strictly postural lightheadedness with visual darkening, relieved by sitting, after an alpha-blocker increase, is classic orthostatic hypotension.
Considered for presyncope, but the strict postural reproducibility and a sustained BP drop are more consistent with orthostatic hypotension.
Presyncope warrants excluding an arrhythmia, though symptoms strictly on standing with a regular pulse make it less likely.
Positional symptoms can suggest BPPV, but visual darkening/presyncope (not true spinning) with a BP drop points to orthostatic hypotension.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should identify likely iatrogenic orthostatic hypotension, arrange medication review and fall prevention, and safety-net arrhythmic/traumatic red flags - without over-triaging a stable patient to emergency.

differential.evidence_basis: Postural presyncope with visual darkening relieved by sitting (DI-001), coat-hanger ache (DI-004) and a recent alpha-blocker increase (DI-002), with no chest pain/palpitations/vertigo (DI-005), indicate iatrogenic orthostatic hypotension.
Remains to be excluded with orthostatic vitals and an ECG, but the strictly postural pattern and regular pulse make it less likely.
The reproducible postural pattern with a sustained BP drop is not vasovagal.
Presyncope with visual darkening and a BP drop, not rotational vertigo, argues against BPPV.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is iatrogenic orthostatic hypotension: withhold the tamsulosin, hydrate/liberalise salt temporarily, teach counter-manoeuvres, reduce fall hazards, and arrange prompt GP review with orthostatic vitals and an ECG.

differential.diagnosis: Iatrogenic orthostatic hypotension - presumptive, pending in-person orthostatic vitals, an ECG, and medication review
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Confirmatory active-stand vitals and injury/ECG assessment need in-person review, but the offending drug can be withheld and fall-prevention advised now.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is likely iatrogenic orthostatic hypotension - withhold the drug, hydrate, counter-manoeuvres and fall prevention with prompt GP review, and safety-net syncope-with-injury/chest pain/palpitations.

red_flags.description: Recurrent postural presyncope with visual darkening and a near-collapse (fall risk)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Recurrent presyncope on standing in an older person carries an immediate risk of a traumatic fall (fracture, head injury), independent of the cause.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A subsequent fall causes a fracture or head injury, especially living alone with home hazards.

red_flags.description: Recent alpha-blocker (tamsulosin) dose increase - reversible iatrogenic cause
red_flags.clinical_significance: Identifying the offending drug allows a simple reversible intervention (withhold/adjust) that typically resolves the problem within 24-48 hours.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to identify the drug cause perpetuates the falls risk.

red_flags.description: Cardiac/traumatic red flags (chest pain, palpitations/irregular pulse, syncope with head strike) - ABSENT but must be safety-netted
red_flags.clinical_significance: Their absence supports a benign orthostatic mechanism and community management; if any develop, an arrhythmic/ischaemic or traumatic cause needs emergency assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If a red flag develops and is not acted on, a serious cardiac or head-injury event could be missed.

red_flags.description: A sustained orthostatic BP drop (>30/15 mmHg on active-stand testing) on clinician measurement
red_flags.clinical_significance: The confirmatory active-stand blood-pressure drop is a clinician-measured finding; a home cuff during the episode is unreliable and orthostatic vitals require standardised in-person measurement.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires clinician orthostatic vitals; the correct action is prompt in-person confirmation.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not eliciting the recent tamsulosin increase misses a simple reversible cause and perpetuates the falls risk.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-triaging a benign orthostatic cause to emergency
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Strictly postural presyncope relieved by sitting, with a clear drug cause and no red flags, does not need an emergency ambulance - medication review and fall prevention are the priorities.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Missing an arrhythmic or traumatic complication
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The AI Doctor must still screen for and safety-net chest pain, palpitations and a hard fall with head injury.
Home hazards (loose rugs, clutter) and living alone must be addressed to prevent a traumatic fall while the drug washes out.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Standardised active-stand orthostatic blood-pressure and heart-rate measurement
Assessment for injuries from the near-fall
A 12-lead ECG to exclude an arrhythmia
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Advise withholding the tamsulosin now given the clear temporal link, increase oral fluids (about 2 L/day) and temporarily liberalise salt, teach counter-manoeuvres (sit on the edge of the bed for two minutes and pump the calves before standing), reduce home fall hazards (remove loose rugs, use a stable chair), and arrange prompt GP review (within 24-48 hours) for orthostatic vitals, an ECG and alternative prostate management - with an ambulance (000) if he has a hard fall with a head injury, or develops chest pain, breathlessness or an actual blackout.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Either over-triaging a benign iatrogenic orthostatic problem to emergency, or failing to identify the drug cause and provide fall-prevention (risking a serious fall), would be errors; an arrhythmic or traumatic complication must not be missed.
